_notes: Default starting weights, not validated for any individual user. Edit the 'max' values to change scoring emphasis – they should sum to 100. See README.md for guidance before changing these on day one: with no outcome history yet, you have no basis to know your own weighting should differ from the default. Let the recalibration agent (see SKILL.md) suggest changes once you have enough outcome data, rather than guessing upfront.

jd_fit.description: How precisely the candidate's credentials map to the stated requirements: seniority level, years of experience, specific must-have skills, domain/sector fit. Scored by deriving the candidate's own competency clusters from their CV, then matching the JD's primary function against them - not a title-keyword match - so a genuine but non-obviously-titled fit isn't structurally undervalued.

recency_modifier._notes: A discount only, never a bonus - fresh evidence isn't a reason to add points beyond the base fit judgement. Applied only when the JD names something explicitly currency-sensitive (a specific tool, current market dynamics, a platform's present capabilities) and the candidate's best evidence for it is old and unreinforced by anything recent. Never applied to durable leadership/institutional credentials, and never triggered by a planned, explained career break on its own - self-directed work during a break counts as recent evidence wherever it's relevant.

seniority.description: Alignment between the candidate's level and the role's level. Asymmetric by design: applying below one's level is rarely penalised; a severe stretch upward is the one scenario that reliably kills callback odds.

competition.description: Estimated size of the applicant pool, driven primarily by company scale/profile, not role seniority. The component most sensitive to getting company facts right, which is why the live-search verification agent exists.

comp.description: Alignment against the candidate's on-target-earnings (OTE) floor from preferences.md – base salary plus target bonus/variable comp, equity explicitly excluded from the number (see equity_handling below). Scored on a sliding scale, not a step function: at or above floor is max marks; below floor slides down linearly and hits zero only at max_shortfall_pct below, so a role 10% under floor scores meaningfully higher than one 45% under. If the JD doesn't state a figure, attempt a live-search-informed estimate (same discipline as competition) before falling back to unestimable_default.

shortfall_scoring._notes: max_shortfall_pct: the shortfall (as a fraction of the floor) at which comp hits 0 - e.g. 0.5 means a role paying 50%+ below floor scores 0, while one 10% below floor scores 8/10 (10 * (1 - 0.10/0.5)). Formula: comp = round(clamp(10 * (1 - shortfall_pct / max_shortfall_pct), 0, 10)), where shortfall_pct = max(0, (floor - offered) / floor). unestimable_default: the score when comp is neither stated nor reasonably estimable even via live search - a deliberate middle value, not the max score, since 'genuinely unknown' should score worse than 'confirmed good' and better than 'confirmed below floor' rather than defaulting to the best possible outcome.

comp.equity_handling: Equity, if mentioned, is never folded into the numeric comp score - too unreliable to value consistently across companies and stages. Note it separately as a qualitative factor in the score rationale/caveats instead.

blockers.description: Right to work, geography, visa, or other confirmed hard requirements the candidate cannot meet.

tiers._notes: Approximate bands, not a hard formula – a score sitting right on a boundary can round either way on judgement, this isn't meant to be applied with more precision than the rubric itself has. Kept here rather than hardcoded in SKILL.md so bands can be tuned the same way component weights can. Tier 4/5 exist for completeness (a genuinely poor fit should say so plainly, not get rounded up into Tier 3) even though this repo's own example dataset only currently has Tier 1-3 examples.

recalibration._notes: The recalibration agent will not propose any weight change below these thresholds – it will say plainly that there isn't enough data yet. Even at the threshold, treat any suggestion as a weak signal, not a validated finding. These numbers are a deliberately conservative floor, not a target to rush toward. 'Positive'/'negative' here are derived from status via scripts/_status.py – reaching interview stage counts as positive even if the process later ends in rejection or withdrawal. min_logged_outcomes_joint gates the joint (multi-component) model specifically – a joint model needs proportionally more data than a single-component comparison to avoid unstable coefficients, so its threshold sits above min_logged_outcomes rather than reusing it.

pipeline_hygiene._notes: When regenerating the dashboard or reviewing the pipeline, the skill checks applied/awaiting_recruiter/interviewing applications with no activity since status_date past this many days, and proposes marking them assumed_rejected – never sets it silently. 21 days is a deliberately generous default; tighten it if your own experience says otherwise.
